free_text: Proton reaction cross section measurements on stable
and neutron-rich nuclei as a probe of the nucleon-
nucleus interaction.

University of Santiago de Compostela.
The empirical formula
for reaction cross section.
Macroscopic
phenomenological potential CH89.
Macroscopic
phenomenological potential GLOBAL.
Optical model code
ESIC.
Last checking has been done.
A cryogenic liquid hydrogen target was built. The
target itself is formed of two parts, 0.5 and 1-cm
thick, respectivily, each of them having a diameter of
2-cm.  The windows are 4.4 micrometers thick havar
foils.  The total thickness of the windows is 7
mg/cm**2, compared to 70 mg/cm**2 of liquid hydrogen
for the 1-cm thick target.  The average target
thickness was found to be equal to 1.12+-0.01 cm for
the 1-cm thick target and to 0.607+-0.006 cm for the
0.5-cm target.
Macroscopic calculations. The optical model.
Square of the strong absorption radii has been deduced
from the reaction cross section derived from Kox
parametrization of the reaction cross section.
The datum is produced by Kox empirical
parametrization.
Downstream of the target the
ions were counted and identified by del'ta-E-E method
in a telescope, located 6.5-cm behind the target and
composed of a thin microstrip, position sensitive, Si
detector, surface 50*50-mm**2, thickness
500-micrometers, a standard semiconductor Si(Li)
detector, surface 50*50-mm**2, thickness
3500-micrometers and a thick CsI scintillator, surface
50*50-mm**2, thickness 4-cm.
The light fragments were identified by using the energy
loss measured in the Si and Si(Li) detectors and the
residual energy in the CsI detector, whereas the
heavier fragments did not reach the latter and were
therefore identified by their energy loss measured in
the Si detector an residual energy in the Si(Li)
detector.
GANIL accelerator complex.
The incident ions were counted and identified by
the del'Ta-e-tof method. Their time-of-flight was
measured with respect to the Rf of the cyclotron with
a position sensitive microchannel plate detector.
Finally a 2-mm thick plastic scintillator, with a 13-mm
diameter hole was used as an active collimator.
The secondary beams were produced by
fragmentation of a 60 MeV/nucleon Ca-48 primary beam,
delivered by the GANIL accelerator complex, and
incident on a 0.5 mm thick Be production target, backed
by a Ta layer of 250-micrometers.
The total error on the experimental values is
about 5%.  It includes the error on the determination
of the target thickness which is of the order of 1% and
the error on the determination of the reaction
probability, which is mainly depending on the
definition of the graphic cuts for the subtraction of
reactions.  The statistical errors are negligible.
